---
title: "FAOSTATdb — Database Schema"
subtitle: "How the tables in a built `faostat.duckdb` relate to one another"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    theme: cosmo
---

## Overview

A FAOSTATdb database is organized around three kinds of objects:

1. **Fact tables** — one per FAOSTAT dataset: `data_qcl`, `data_fbs`, `data_ae`, …
   (`data_<code>`, lower-cased). They hold the measurements, keeping only
   dimension **codes** (`area_code`, `item_code`, …) plus `value` and `flag_code`.
2. **Dimension tables** — shared across datasets: `dim_area`, `dim_item`,
   `dim_element`, `dim_year`, `dim_flag`, … Each is keyed by
   `(dataset_code, <stem>_code)` because FAOSTAT codes are **not** globally unique
   across domains. They hold the deduplicated labels / alternate codes.
3. **Labelled views** — one per dataset: `view_<code>_labelled`. Each is the fact
   table with its dimension labels (and flag descriptions) already joined back on,
   so you can query without writing joins.

Around those sit the **provenance tables** (`faostat_dataset`, `faostat_build`,
`faostat_column_mapping`, `faostat_constant_column`) and a clearly non-source
**enrichment** table (`area_classification`), built by default (disable with
`--no-enrich-areas`).

## Entity–relationship diagram

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    faostat_dataset ||--o{ data_CODE : "one fact table per dataset"
    faostat_dataset ||--o{ faostat_column_mapping : "records renames"
    faostat_dataset ||--o{ faostat_constant_column : "records dropped constants"
    faostat_build ||--o{ faostat_dataset : "a build imports datasets"

    data_CODE }o--|| dim_area : "dataset_code, area_code"
    data_CODE }o--|| dim_item : "dataset_code, item_code"
    data_CODE }o--|| dim_element : "dataset_code, element_code"
    data_CODE }o--|| dim_year : "dataset_code, year"
    data_CODE }o--|| dim_flag : "dataset_code, flag_code"
    data_CODE ||--|| view_CODE_labelled : "labels joined back"
    dim_area ||--o| area_classification : "enrichment, on by default (area_code)"

    faostat_dataset {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR dataset_name
        VARCHAR date_update
        BIGINT  file_rows_declared
        BIGINT  rows_imported
        BIGINT  source_csv_rows
        VARCHAR archive_sha256
        VARCHAR import_status
    }
    faostat_build {
        VARCHAR build_id PK
        TIMESTAMP completed_at
        VARCHAR metadata_snapshot_sha256
        VARCHAR duckdb_version
    }
    data_CODE {
        INTEGER area_code FK
        VARCHAR item_code FK
        INTEGER element_code FK
        INTEGER year FK
        VARCHAR unit
        DOUBLE  value
        VARCHAR flag_code FK
    }
    dim_area {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR area_code PK
        VARCHAR area_code_m49
        VARCHAR area_label
    }
    dim_item {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR item_code PK
        VARCHAR item_label
    }
    dim_element {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR element_code PK
        VARCHAR element_label
    }
    dim_year {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR year PK
        VARCHAR year_code
    }
    dim_flag {
        VARCHAR dataset_code PK
        VARCHAR flag_code PK
        VARCHAR flag_description
    }
    view_CODE_labelled {
        VARCHAR dataset_code
        VARCHAR area_code
        VARCHAR area_label
        VARCHAR item_code
        VARCHAR item_label
        VARCHAR value
        VARCHAR flag_code
        VARCHAR flag_description
    }
    area_classification {
        INTEGER area_code PK
        VARCHAR area_label
        BOOLEAN is_country
        INTEGER valid_from
        INTEGER valid_to
    }
```

## Reading the relationships

- **`faostat_dataset` ⟶ `data_<code>`.** One metadata row per dataset; one fact
  table per dataset. Table names are dynamic, so there is no SQL foreign-key
  constraint — the link is the `dataset_code` (`data_qcl` ↔ the row where
  `dataset_code = 'QCL'`).
- **`data_<code>` ⟶ `dim_*`.** Fact tables store only codes. To get labels, join a
  dimension on **both** `dataset_code` **and** the `<stem>_code`. For example, for
  `data_qcl` the dataset code is `'QCL'`:

  ```sql
  SELECT a.area_label, TRY_CAST(d.value AS DOUBLE) AS value
  FROM data_qcl d
  JOIN dim_area a ON a.dataset_code = 'QCL' AND a.area_code = d.area_code;
  ```

- **`view_<code>_labelled`.** Precomputes exactly those joins. Prefer it for
  interactive/dataframe use — no joins to write.
- **`dim_flag`.** Labels flag codes with their descriptions (loaded from the
  archive's flag legend). Also joined into the labelled view.
- **`faostat_column_mapping` / `faostat_constant_column`.** Audit trails — the
  raw→normalized column renames, and any column that was constant across a whole
  dataset (dropped from the fact table, its single value recorded here). Both are
  keyed by `dataset_code`.
- **`area_classification`.** Built **by default** (disable with `--no-enrich-areas`)
  and **not** source FAOSTAT data, kept in its own table so it is never confused
  with what FAOSTAT published. Both columns come from one committed, hand-curated
  file — `faostatdb/area_classification.csv` — matched to `dim_area` by name:
  `is_country` is `true` for a single country or territory (former single states
  like the USSR or Sudan (former) included) and `false` for a group of
  countries/areas (continents, unions, income groups, FAO fishing areas, and
  rollups such as "China" = mainland+Taiwan+HK+Macao or "Belgium-Luxembourg");
  `valid_from`/`valid_to` (disable with `--no-enrich-history`) carry documented
  political transition years (USSR → 1991, South Sudan 2011 →, …) and are NULL
  otherwise (we never guess a date). Editing the CSV and rebuilding is all it takes
  to change a classification — see the README for the full contract.

## Rendering this document

This is a [Quarto](https://quarto.org/) document. With Quarto installed:

```bash
quarto render docs/schema.qmd          # -> docs/schema.html
```

The same diagram is embedded in the project [README](../README.md); GitHub renders
the ```` ```mermaid ```` block there directly, so you don't need Quarto just to see
it.
